title: Generation of heat pulses in mesoscopic conductors using light fields
authors: Pedro Portugal, Riku Tuovinen, Christian Flindt
abstract: We propose to generate heat pulses in mesoscopic conductors using light fields. In contrast to single-electron excitations such as levitons, which are created by accurate voltage drives, our approach relies on modulating the temperature of an electronic reservoir. To this end, we show that the interactions with a light field can induce a controllable time-dependent temperature in an electrode. The temperature modulations generate charge-neutral heat pulses that can be emitted into a mesoscopic conductor and detected in the outputs. We illustrate our approach by evaluating the time-dependent currents and their fluctuations using a tight-binding model of two electronic reservoirs connected by a quantum point contact. Our work establishes a route towards on-demand caloritronics, where energy rather than charge carries quantum information, and it paves the way for probing time-resolved heat transport and quantum coherence in thermally driven conductors.
summary: This paper proposes a method to generate controllable, charge-neutral heat pulses in mesoscopic conductors using light fields. By modulating the electronic temperature via light-induced changes in tunnel coupling, the authors establish a framework for energy-based quantum information processing. The results show that these pulses can be precisely controlled and even described using the Schwarzian derivative.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for a way to generate and control time-dependent, charge-neutral heat pulses in mesoscopic conductors to enable 'on-demand caloritronics'.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a high-frequency light field can modulate the electronic temperature in a reservoir, creating traveling heat pulses that carry energy without net electric current.
method: The study uses a tight-binding model, full counting statistics (FCS), and Floquet scattering theory, supplemented by Conformal Field Theory to derive quantum corrections.
model_or_system: A 1D mesoscopic conductor consisting of two electronic reservoirs connected by a quantum point contact (QPC), modeled as a tight-binding chain irradiated by a monochromatic light field.
key_observables: Time-dependent heat current (Ih), electric current (Iq), heat noise, and the second cumulant of transferred charge and energy.
important_parameters: High-frequency limit (light frequency Omega much larger than tunnel coupling gamma), driving amplitude alpha, Fermi velocity vF, and base temperature T0.
main_assumptions: The light field is treated in the high-frequency limit (Omega >> gamma) with a spatially uniform field approximation (wavelength lambda >> lattice spacing a).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of heat pulse generation; Figure 2 shows the effect of light on band structure; Figure 3 displays time-dependent heat current under constant light; Figure 4 and 5 show the evolution of charge and heat noise/cumulants.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of heat pulse generation, describes the tight-binding model and light-matter interaction, presents the derivation of effective temperature modulation, evaluates observables using FCS, and concludes with the implications for caloritronics.
why_interesting: It explores the intersection of non-equilibrium thermodynamics and quantum transport, specifically how time-dependent driving can be used to manipulate energy flow (caloritronics) independently of charge flow.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Generation of heat pulses in a mesoscopic conductor. (a) A high-frequency light field generates a heat pulse, which travels at the Fermi velocity vF along a one-dimensional con- ductor of length L. The pulse is charge-neutral and only car- ries heat. (b) The pulse produces a time-dependent heat cur- rent, Ih(t), at the quantum point contact but no electric cur- rent, Iq(t). Details of the calculation are described in Sec. VI.

FIG. 2. Changing the Fermi velocity using a light field. (a) The standard cosine band structure of a tight-binding chain for different values of the tunnel coupling γ. The tunnel coupling together with the distance between the atoms in the chain determines the Fermi velocity vF = 2γa. We consider a half-filled chain, such that states up until ϵ(k) = 0 are occupied. (b) Close to the Fermi energy, the dispersion relation is linear, and a continuum description of the tight-binding chain applies. A fast external light field allows us to change the tunnel coupling γ(t) in parts of the system and thereby the Fermi velocity.

FIG. 3. Heat current in response to a constant light-induced temperature difference. The dashed lines are the predic- tions based on Eq. (24), where, for each curve, the driv- ing amplitude α0 has been chosen to give a specific value of T. The other parameters are 2n = 1000, Ω= 2.5γ, and kBT0 = 0.025ℏγ/n, and we have defined I0 h = k2 BT 2 0 /ℏand chosen γqpc, so that D = 1/2. The first 225 atoms of the left electrode are irradiated by the light field.

FIG. 4. Electric noise in response to a constant light-induced temperature difference. (a) The time-dependent second cumulant of the transferred charge corresponding to the results in Fig. 3. The long-time behavior of the second cumulant determines the zero-frequency noise of the current. (b) Zero-frequency noise of the current for different light-induced temperatures. The dashed line is the prediction based on Eq. (27). The parameters are the same as in Fig. 3, and we have defined S0 q = e2kBT0/ℏ.

FIG. 5. Heat noise in response to a constant light-induced temperature difference. (a) The time-dependent second cumulant of the transferred heat corresponding to the results in Fig. 3. The long-time behavior of the second cumulant determines the zero-frequency heat noise. (b) Zero-frequency heat noise for different light-induced temperatures. The dashed line is the prediction based on Eq. (28). The parameters are the same as in Fig. 3, and we have defined S0 h = k3 BT 3 0 /ℏ.
